%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-plan" data-visual-plan-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
# B.5-5 Improper Rational Functions — The Special Case m = n

> **Section Objective:** Learn the fast shortcut for partial fractions when the numerator and denominator have the same degree — a pattern that appears regularly on exams.

Consider \ (F(x) = \frac{3x^2 + 9x - 20}{x^2 + x - 6}\). The top and bottom both have degree 2, so this looks "improper" — and students often freeze here, reaching for long division. Don't. When \(m = n\), the textbook gives you a clean shortcut: write the expansion exactly like a proper partial fraction, but add one extra constant term at the front. That's the only difference. In this section you will learn to recognize the \(m = n\) pattern, write the correct decomposition form immediately, and compute all coefficients using the same substitution formula you already know.

%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-meta" data-visual-kind="generate_image" data-teaching-role="concept_anchor" data-visual-use-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
*📊 Left: the standard proper-case form with no constant term. Right: the m = n improper case — identical structure plus one extra constant \(b_n\) at the front. Everything else is the same.*
![Chart](/generated/fig-1777196036194-l2hc2tss.png)

## 1. The Rule When m = n

When the numerator and denominator of \(F(x)\) have the **same degree**, the partial fraction expansion is almost identical to the proper case — with exactly one addition: an extra constant term \(b_n\) placed at the front, where \(b_n\) is the leading coefficient of the numerator (assuming the denominator is monic, i.e., its leading coefficient is 1).

### KEY RULE

Write the decomposition as: constant \(b_n\) first, then the usual fractional terms for each factor.

> **Exam Note:** Do not waste time performing a full polynomial long division for this special case. The textbook form gives you the constant directly — it is simply the leading numerator coefficient.

The fractional coefficients \(k_r\) are found using **exactly the same substitution formula** as in the proper case. Repeated or quadratic factors are still handled by the earlier proper-function procedures — nothing changes there.

$$F(x)=\frac{b_nx^n+b_{n-1}x^{n-1}+\cdots+b_1x+b_0}{x^n+a_{n-1}x^{n-1}+\cdots+a_1x+a_0}=b_n+\frac{k_1}{x-\lambda_1}+\frac{k_2}{x-\lambda_2}+\cdots+\frac{k_n}{x-\lambda_n}$$
*When the numerator and denominator have equal degree, the partial fraction form is the standard proper-case expansion plus one extra constant term \(b_n\), the leading coefficient of the numerator.*

$$k_r=\left.(x-\lambda_r)F(x)\right|_{x=\lambda_r}$$
*Each coefficient \(k_r\) is found by multiplying \(F(x)\) by the matching linear factor \((x - \lambda_r)\) and then substituting the corresponding root \(x = \lambda_r\), exactly as in the proper case.*

## 2. Worked Example

Let \(F(x) = \dfrac{3x^2 + 9x - 20}{(x-2)(x+3)}\).

**Step 1 — Recognize m = n.** Both numerator and denominator have degree \(n = 2\). The denominator is monic (leading coefficient 1), so the extra constant is \(b_n = 3\). Write:
$$
F(x) = 3 + \frac{k_1}{x-2} + \frac{k_2}{x+3}
$$

**Step 2 — Find \(k_1\).** Multiply by \((x-2)\) and substitute \(x = 2\):
$$
k_1 = \frac{3(2)^2 + 9(2) - 20}{2+3} = \frac{12+18-20}{5} = \frac{10}{5} = 2
$$

**Step 3 — Find \(k_2\).** Multiply by \((x+3)\) and substitute \(x = -3\):
$$
k_2 = \frac{3(-3)^2 + 9(-3) - 20}{-3-2} = \frac{27-27-20}{-5} = \frac{-20}{-5} = 4
$$

**Step 4 — Final answer:**
$$
F(x) = 3 + \frac{2}{x-2} + \frac{4}{x+3}
$$

> **Exam Warning:** The most common mistake is writing only \(\dfrac{2}{x-2} + \dfrac{4}{x+3}\) and forgetting the constant 3 entirely — that costs full marks.

Think of the \(m = n\) case like packing for a trip where you already know the routine — same bags, same process — except this time you also have one carry-on to grab before you start. The extra constant \(b_n\) is that carry-on: pick it up first, set it aside, then find all the other coefficients exactly as you normally would. The carry-on does not change how you pack the rest.

---
**📌 Key Takeaways**
- When m = n, recognize the improper case immediately by comparing numerator and denominator degrees.
- Add one extra constant term \(b_n\) (the leading numerator coefficient) at the front of the expansion.
- All fractional coefficients \(k_r\) are still found using the same cover-up substitution formula.

*In the next section we will look at a modified partial fraction form used for inverse z-transform work.*
